entry.title: A Post-Quantum Path for BIP 324
entry.summary:
The discussion initiated by Olaoluwa Osuntokun raises pertinent questions regarding the potential shift from BIP 324 to TLS 1.3 for encrypting peer-to-peer (P2P) connections in Bitcoin's network. The primary motivation behind considering TLS 1.3 hinges on its ability to make Bitcoin P2P connections more indistinguishable from other TLS communications, thereby reducing the likelihood of unintentional blocking by firewalls due to unrecognized protocols. Although this does not guarantee complete privacy—as TLS encrypted Bitcoin traffic can still be differentiated through analysis of timing and size—it represents a step toward enhancing the invisibility of these connections.
Further benefits of adopting TLS 1.3 include the utilization of QUIC for faster data relay and the implementation of advanced features like oblivious HTTP and QUIC tunnels. These technologies could potentially facilitate private relay protocols and similar functionalities, thereby bolstering the efficiency and security of Bitcoin's network communication. Moreover, the widespread adoption and scrutiny of TLS 1.3 by the cybersecurity community ensure that there is no need for additional development or maintenance efforts specific to this protocol within the Bitcoin core infrastructure.
These considerations present a compelling case for integrating TLS 1.3, which would not only streamline certain operational aspects but also leverage existing, robust security mechanisms widely recognized in the digital realm. However, concerns about adding TLS 1.3 as a complex dependency to bitcoin-core remain, necessitating further discussion and evaluation within the community.
